step_by_step_thinking:
I agree with option A — acute intermittent porphyria — because it is the only condition among the listed choices that has a documented, direct pathophysiological link to sulfa drug ingestion and presents with exactly the same constellation of symptoms: abdominal pain, altered mental status, and seizures. This matches the classic "triad" seen in acute intermittent porphyria triggered by medication such as sulfonamides.

Answer: A

---

Agree. Acute intermittent porphyria is supported by both the known pharmacogenomic sensitivity to sulfa drugs and the precise match of clinical symptoms (abdominal pain, incoherence, seizures), which form the diagnostic triad.
